ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for paneer tikka; closest_parent is cottage cheese food product (FOODON_03311737) as paneer is a fresh Indian cottage cheese.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for paneer tikka; A0CKP (Cheese, fresh unripened) is the closest defensible parent for paneer as a fresh unripened cheese product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 refers to paneer as a substance; no specific code for paneer tikka preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of paneer tikka consists of approximately 150 g, comprising several cubes of grilled marinated paneer served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Tikka — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), adapted with USDA FoodData Central reference values for marinated grilled paneer
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) and tandoori-style preparations. Values for paneer tikka are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer base values (protein ~18 g, fat ~20 g per 100 g) adjusted for marinade ingredients (yogurt, spices, mustard oil) and tandoor grilling, which reduces moisture. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cottage cheese (full fat) and grilled preparations used as cross-reference. Composite estimate is consistent with published Indian dietetic literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of paneer and Indian dairy-based preparations — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate from published GI values for fresh cheese and high-protein low-carbohydrate Indian foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for paneer tikka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 27 based on the GI of fresh paneer (cheese) and analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate dairy foods reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., full-fat cheese GI ~27, yogurt-marinated preparations GI 20–35). The low carbohydrate content (~5.2 g per 100 g) and high protein and fat content strongly attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (27 × 5.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.1, confirming very low GL. This estimate is consistent with Augustin et al. (2015) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~630 mg per 150 g serving) from marinade spices, salt, and mustard
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving (150 g) and request reduced salt in the marinade, as a single serving contributes approximately 630 mg sodium, representing about 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~27) and low glycemic load (~2.1 per serving) due to high protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer tikka is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its very low GI (~27) and low GL (~2.1 per 150 g serving) produce minimal postprandial blood glucose elevation; however, the high saturated fat content (~11 g per 100 g) warrants moderation in those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~11.2 g per 100 g) from full-fat paneer, contributing to LDL cholesterol elevation risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume paneer tikka in moderation due to its high saturated fat content (~11.2 g per 100 g), which may raise LDL cholesterol; choosing smaller portions (100 g) and pairing with high-fibre vegetables can help mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Tikka is a popular North Indian vegetarian appetiser made from cubes of fresh Indian cottage cheese (paneer) that are marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically containing mustard, turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then grilled in a traditional clay tandoor oven at high heat. The result is a dish with a lightly charred, smoky exterior and a soft, creamy interior. Paneer tikka has a rich, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a firm yet yielding texture. It is commonly served as a starter or snack with mint-coriander chutney and sliced raw onions. Nutritionally, paneer tikka is high in protein (~17.5 g per 100 g) and fat (~19.5 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated), and low in carbohydrates (~5.2 g per 100 g), making it a high-satiety, low-glycaemic food. Its very low glycemic index (~27) makes it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The high calcium content (~480 mg per 100 g) supports bone health. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) requires attention for hypertensive individuals, and the high saturated fat warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, paneer tikka is widely available at North Indian restaurants and is a popular choice among vegetarians and those seeking high-protein Indian cuisine.
common_user_queries: How many calories in paneer tikka?
Is paneer tikka good for diabetics?
Is paneer tikka high in protein?
What is paneer tikka made of?
Is paneer tikka suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer base data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference, as no exact entry for paneer tikka (marinated, tandoor-grilled) exists in a single approved primary source. The adaptation is well-supported by published Indian dietetic literature and is internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using fresh cheese and high-protein dairy analogues. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a precise match for the complete prepared dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) paneer values and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, adjusted for marinade ingredients and tandoor grilling moisture loss. No single approved source provides an exact entry for paneer tikka as a complete prepared dish. 2. GI is an analogous estimate (~27) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for fresh cheese and high-protein low-carbohydrate dairy foods; no direct GI measurement for paneer tikka was found. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN paneer composition and USDA SR Legacy full-fat cottage cheese data; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. 4. The food_name has been normalised from 'Paneer Tikka(Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical 'Paneer Tikka'; the chef recommendation qualifier is a menu descriptor and not part of the canonical food identity. 5. This dish is classified as North Indian cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. 6. Halal status is listed as true as paneer tikka contains no meat or alcohol; however, halal certification of the specific restaurant should be verified independently. 7. The FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code for paneer tikka as a prepared dish was found in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of the processing date.

Computed glycemic_load_per_serving as 2.1 for 150 g serving.
